slides.title: Functions That Take Anything
slides.body: What if you want a function that works with **2 arguments today and 10 tomorrow**? `*args` and `**kwargs` let your functions accept as many inputs as needed — no rewriting required.
slides.tip: These are not special keywords — the magic is in the * and ** symbols, not the names 'args' or 'kwargs'.

slides.title: *args Packs Extras into a Tuple
slides.body: Prefix a parameter with `*` and Python bundles every extra positional argument into a **tuple** inside your function.
slides.tip: args is always a tuple — immutable and ordered, just like the values you passed in.

slides.title: **kwargs Packs Extras into a Dict
slides.body: Prefix a parameter with `**` and Python collects every extra **keyword argument** into a dictionary — keys are the names, values are the values.
slides.tip: kwargs is a plain dict — you can loop over it, look things up, or pass it around like any other dict.

slides.title: Mixing Them — Order Is Everything
slides.body: You can combine all four kinds of parameters, but Python requires a **strict order**: regular params → `*args` → keyword-only → `**kwargs`.
slides.tip: Putting **kwargs before *args causes a SyntaxError — Python is strict about this.

slides.title: You've Got Flexible Functions!
slides.body: With `*args` and `**kwargs` you can write functions that handle **any number of inputs** — positional or named. The same tools also let you **unpack** lists and dicts into any function call with `*` and `**`.
slides.tip: Next time you see *args or **kwargs in someone else's code, you'll know exactly what's happening.
